benchmarks.note: Average benchmark used for comparison messaging.

sources.label: Showers
sources.title: Showerheads
sources.figure: Standard showerheads use 2.5 gallons per minute, about 9.5 litres per minute.

sources.label: Toilet flushing
sources.title: Residential Toilets
sources.figure: Older toilets can use as much as 6 gallons per flush and WaterSense toilets use 1.28 gallons per flush or less.

sources.label: Brushing teeth and running taps
sources.title: Bathroom Faucets
sources.figure: Standard bathroom sink faucets flow at 2.2 gallons per minute, about 8.3 litres per minute.

sources.label: Dishwasher loads
sources.title: Dishwashers
sources.figure: ENERGY STAR highlights water-saving improvements and lifetime water savings for efficient dishwashers; AquaSave uses a conservative 15-litre per-load planning estimate for modern efficient cycles.
sources.note: Planning estimate anchored to ENERGY STAR efficiency guidance, not a single mandated litres-per-load standard.

sources.label: Laundry loads
sources.title: Clothes Washers
sources.figure: ENERGY STAR clothes washers use about 30% less water than regular washers; AquaSave uses planning estimates by machine type to compare efficient vs. less efficient loads.
sources.note: The site uses comparative estimates because ENERGY STAR publishes efficiency deltas rather than one universal litres-per-load value.

sources.label: Baths
sources.title: Your Better Bathroom
sources.figure: Bathrooms are the largest indoor water users; AquaSave uses a 135-litre planning estimate for a full bath to model household impact.
sources.note: Bath volume varies widely by tub size and fill level, so this is an explicit model assumption.

sources.label: Garden watering
sources.title: Sprinkler Spruce-Up and Landscaping Tips
sources.figure: EPA outdoor guidance focuses on avoiding overspray, leaks, and unnecessary irrigation; AquaSave uses a 15-litre per-minute hose estimate for simple household planning.
sources.note: Outdoor watering rates depend heavily on nozzle type and pressure, so this is an explicit model assumption.
